summary: Cathy O'Neil shares a personal update about her running progress, having completed her second 5K race. She improved her time by 5 seconds (from 39:00 to 38:55) and found the race easier than her first attempt in February. She's now training for a sprint triathlon in May, combining running, biking, and swimming. The post is deliberately lighthearted and self-deprecating, celebrating small victories and the joy of simply participating rather than competing.
key_points: Completed second 5K with modest improvement (5 seconds faster, 0.21% improvement)
Training philosophy focuses on finishing without stopping rather than speed or placement
Preparing for a sprint triathlon by building stamina with multi-activity days
Finds joy in being a slow runner because it makes faster runners feel good about themselves
Running serves as an outlet for 'urban aggression' and she's grateful for her health
money_quotes: I told you I'm boring now, so here's proof.
By the end of the race I'm kind of the last always-runner and ahead of most of the sometimes-walkers.
For those interested, that's 0.21% faster. Did I mention that I'm a slow runner? I'm also a very consistently slow runner.
I honestly enjoy being that slow runner whom everyone passes, because it makes them feel really fast, and I'm happy for them, and I don't mind at all being slow.
I'm just grateful to be healthy.
It's nice sometimes to do something easy instead of forcing yourself to do something hard.
key_insight: There's value in showing up and finishing at your own pace rather than competing, and gratitude for health matters more than performance metrics.
